query: pathophysiology of hyaline membrane disease and relationship between functional residual capacity and closing volume

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Functional Residual Capacity -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: A reduced FRC can result in shunts and atelectasis. This occurs when the FRC decreases below the lung's closing capacity, the volume at which the respiratory bronchioles collapse.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Functional Residual Capacity -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Reduced lung volumes result in reduced FRC. Low lung volumes result in less alveolar tension pulling the lung airways open, and the airway narrowing results in increased airway resistance.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Functional Residual Capacity -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: With obstructive diseases such as emphysema, the FRC is increased. With emphysema, the lungs become increasingly compliant due to alveolar destruction. As the alveoli are destroyed, air is trapped in the lungs, and TLC is increased. The increased volume and lung tissue compliance causes the chest wall to expand, hence, the typical barrel chest seen in those with emphysema.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Functional Residual Capacity -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The lung's compliance depends on the elastic recoil of the lung tissue. Decreases in this result in an increased FRC.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Functional Residual Capacity -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: In restrictive diseases, the TLC decreases, resulting in decreased FRC, and the lung tissues or chest wall expansion is limited or restricted. One example of restriction due to chest wall issues is severe kyphosis or weakness of spinal bones. Kyphosis is described elsewhere. [9] Restrictive pathology can also be due to lung tissues; 1 example is idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis. This disease is described elsewhere. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Functional Residual Capacity -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The FRC is affected by conditions that affect lung compliance, a combination of the lung's inward elastic recoil and the chest wall's outward expansion. These include diseases or conditions with changes in lung tissue compliance (emphysema and interstitial lung diseases), decreased chest movements (kyphoscoliosis), or decreased thoracic volume (obesity, pregnancy). Other factors affecting FRC include acute position changes such as lying supine, age, height, and gender.

retrieved_docs.title: Restrictive Lung Disease -- Pathophysiology -- Functional Characteristics
retrieved_docs.content: Functional characteristics of a restrictive pattern in pulmonary function tests (PFTs) include decreased TLC and forced vital capacity (FVC). This latter parameter is the maximum amount of air exhaled after a maximal inhalation and depends on the elasticity of the lung tissue, the anatomy of the thoracic cage, and the function of the respiratory muscles. According to the American Thoracic Society (ATS), the TLC predicted (adjusted for gender, age, height) value can be used for assessing the severity of the restrictive condition: [14] [15] TLC < 80% = Restrictive disease 70-80 % predicted: mild restrictive disease 60-70% predicted: moderate restrictive disease 50-60% predicted: moderately severe restrictive disease < 50% predicted: severe restrictive disease

retrieved_docs.title: Pulmonary Function Tests -- Clinical Significance -- Restrictive Abnormalities
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with kyphoscoliosis, a restrictive ventilatory defect is common on pulmonary function tests. The decline in vital capacity (VC) correlates with the degree of kyphosis. The ventilatory defect manifest by reduced VC and total lung capacity (TLC) with preserved residual volume.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Functional Residual Capacity -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: At FRC, the work to inflate the lungs is the lowest, as the inward and outward lung compliances are balanced.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Transpulmonary Pressure -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: In cases of restrictive lung disease, such as idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis or infiltrative lung diseases, increased effort is necessary for lung expansion due to pathologic lung remodeling. The decrease in lung compliance leads to an overall reduction in lung volumes. For these reasons, transpulmonary pressure becomes increased. On spirometry, this shows as decreased lung compliance, decrease in all lung volumes, including functional residual capacity. Since the reduction of FVC is more significant than the decline in FEV1, the overall FEV1/FVC ratio increases in this setting. [14] [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Functional Residual Capacity -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Pulmonary vascular resistance is a U-shaped combination of alveolar and extra-alveolar vessel resistances. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK554380/ Thus, there are larger resistances at TLC and RV, and the lowest resistance is at the FRC volume.

retrieved_docs.title: Pulmonary Function Tests and Thoracic Cage Abnormalities -- Summary / Explanation -- Lung Volume Measurements
retrieved_docs.content: Two methods are used to measure lung volumes: body plethysmography and gas dilution (nitrogen washout and inert gas dilution). [9] [10] These tests assess the total air the lungs can hold and how well air moves in and out of the lungs. Lung volume measurements include total lung capacity (TLC), functional residual capacity (FRC), and residual volume (RV). The diagnosis of restriction often involves the use of lung volumes. In obstructive lung disease cases, lung volumes are used to evaluate hyperinflation. Other clinical conditions cause changes in lung volumes as well. [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Functional Residual Capacity -- Function -- Age
retrieved_docs.content: As humans age, our pulmonary function also declines due to decreased respiratory muscle mass and tissue elasticity. Loss of elasticity in connective tissue increases the work of breathing; similar to chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) (but to a lesser extent), the air becomes harder to expel, and the lungs do not return to normal size as readily after inspiration. Thus, the FRC increases slightly with age.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Lung Capacity -- Clinical Significance -- Restrictive Lung Disease and Obstructive Lung Disease Effects on Lung Capacity
retrieved_docs.content: The American Thoracic Society/European Respiratory Society (ATS/ERS) defines a restrictive ventilatory defect as a reduced TLC below the 5th percentile of normal predicted value with a normal FEV1/VC ratio. [11] In patients with restrictive lung disease, the underlying pathophysiology associated with reduced TLC is a result of decreased lung compliance and decrease chest wall expansion. These patients cannot expand their lungs fully, resulting in reduce inhalation air volume. Due to loss of lung compliance, the TLC,  FRC, and RV are reduced as seen in Figure 1 Panel C. Some restrictive lung disease include: pectus excavatum, kyphoscoliosis, myasthenia gravis, and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. The ATS/ERS defines obstructive lung disease as a ventilatory defect with an unequal reduction of maximal airflow from the lung compared to the maximum volume of gas that can be removed from the lungs with a FEV1/VC ratio below the 5th percentile of the predicted value. [11] [2] The underlying pathophysiology of obstructive lung diseases results from changes to the airway, lung parenchyma, and pulmonary vasculature resulting in hyperinflation and air trapping within the lungs and decreased air expelled from the lungs during exhalation. On lung volume testing the TLC and the RV is increased as seen in Figure 1 Panel C. Some obstructive lung disease include chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), chronic bronchitis, asthma, and emphysema. Infrequently, a patient may be diagnosed to have mixed obstructive-restrictive lung disease (MORLD). MORLD presents with a decreased TLC on lung volume testing and spirometry testing significant for airway obstruction. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Residual Volume -- Clinical Significance -- Obstructive Lung Disease (OLD)
retrieved_docs.content: Body plethysmography yields a higher FRC in patients with obstructive lung disease than those measured by gas dilution techniques because it includes both well-ventilated and poorly ventilated lung areas. RV is generally the first volume to increase in obstructive lung disease and can be a good measure to evaluate early disease states. Additional findings on spirometry for OLD include decreased forced expiratory volume in the first second of expiration (FEV1), decreased forced vital capacity (FVC), and FEV1/FVC ratio <70% because FEV1 decreases more than FVC. The RV/TLC ratio is used as a measure of resting pulmonary hyperinflation in patients with COPD. An elevated RC/TLC ratio is a significant risk factor for all-cause mortality in COPD patients. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Functional Residual Capacity -- Related Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Lung volumes are followed to track a patient’s respiratory disease. While not routinely used in clinical practice, 1 way to measure residual volume and total lung capacity (TLC) is to measure a person’s FRC. FRC can be measured/calculated using techniques such as the whole body plethysmograph method (based on Boyle’s law) and the helium dilution (based on the law of conservation of mass). [8]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2792
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory distress syndrome is classified as a specific type of lung disorder (اِضطِراب الرِّئَة), indicating that it falls under the broader category of pulmonary disturbances. Recognizing this hierarchical relationship helps clinicians understand that respiratory distress syndrome shares fundamental characteristics with other lung disorders, which can guide accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment strategies, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1701
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult) is a specific adult respiratory condition that is classified under the broader category of اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ (respiratory disorder).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1723
retrieved_docs.content: The term خديج designates infants who have completed 29–30 weeks of gestation, and this developmental stage is directly linked to the occurrence of متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (pulmonary immaturity syndrome). Consequently, babies classified as خديج are at risk for respiratory complications arising from lung immaturity, highlighting the importance of recognizing this gestational age category when assessing and managing neonatal respiratory health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2793
retrieved_docs.content: Apnea (الاِختِنَاق) is the overarching respiratory disturbance that encompasses the broader category of respiratory disorder (اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ). Within this category, the clinical condition described as respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult) represents a more severe manifestation. Thus, adult respiratory failure, insufficiency, and arrest are subtypes of the respiratory disorder, which itself is nested under the primary concept of apnea, illustrating a hierarchical relationship that links a fundamental breathing abnormality to increasingly critical respiratory pathologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 223
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes a hierarchical framework that links gestational age, growth parameters, perinatal infections, and neonatal complications. Completion of 29‑30 weeks of gestation is classified as a preterm (خديج) condition and is considered a liveborn outcome; “liveborn” functions as a broad category that contains newborn disorders (اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة), which are a subclass of the preterm condition. Preterm birth is frequently associated with respiratory distress syndrome (متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة), also termed pulmonary surfactant deficiency syndrome, highlighting the causal link between early gestation and immature lung development. Respiratory distress syndrome is simultaneously classified as a respiratory disorder (اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ) and a lung disorder (اِضطِراب الرِّئَة), and is a member of the neonatal‑and‑newborn disorder set (اضطرابات لدى حديث الولادة, SMQ). In ICD‑10‑AM it maps to the code for اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ under the parent “Duration of gestation,” and within

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 663
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic asthmatic bronchitis is reported to be associated with انسداد (obstruction), indicating that airway narrowing may play a role in the disease’s pathophysiology. Obstructive kidney disease, described as اعتلال الكلية الانسدادي, is likewise linked to انسداد, suggesting that urinary outflow blockage contributes to renal impairment. The concept of انسداد extends to several other urological and systemic conditions: it is associated with انسداد السبيل البولي (urinary tract obstruction), with خانُوق, with رُّكودِ صفْراوِيّ, and it has a direct relationship to انسداد المثانة (bladder obstruction). Together, these relationships highlight انسداد as a central pathological mechanism that can affect the respiratory system, the kidneys, the urinary tract, and additional bodily processes, underscoring the importance of recognizing and managing obstructive phenomena across multiple organ systems in patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: the impact of catheter‑based interventions on patient assessment. Tracheal stenosis (فغر القصبة الهوائية) is classified as a condition within airway management, and vascular edema (الوذمة الوعائية (SMQ)) is listed as a member of intubation and associated with tracheal stenosis, underscoring the need to consider both structural airway obstruction and swelling‑related factors in the evaluation and management of compromised breathing.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1539
retrieved_docs.content: Bard‑Pic syndrome is morphologically associated with obstruction (انسداد), indicating that structural abnormalities characteristic of this syndrome often involve obstructive processes. Chronic asthmatic bronchitis is also associated with obstruction, reflecting the contribution of airway narrowing and mucus plugging to the disease’s clinical picture. The concept of obstruction (انسداد) itself is linked to several specific obstructive conditions: urinary tract obstruction (انسداد السبيل البولي), a yellowish stagnation state (رُّكودِ صفْراوِيّ), the entity عِلَّوس, renal obstruction (موه الكلى), and bladder obstruction (انسداد المثانة). Together, these relationships highlight that both genetic syndromes such as Bard‑Pic and chronic respiratory diseases like asthmatic bronchitis can involve or predispose to a range of localized obstructive pathologies affecting the urinary system, kidneys, bladder, and other tissues, underscoring the importance of recognizing and managing obstruction in diverse clinical contexts.

Collectively, these entities form a network of diagnostic and therapeutic actions centered on the larynx, trachea, bronchi, and lung within the thoracic cavity, organized through parent‑child hierarchies, broader procedural groupings, and anatomical associations that are essential for coordinated patient care in respiratory and thoracic medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1830
retrieved_docs.content: اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة (neonatal disorder) is a broad clinical category that includes specific conditions such as respiratory distress syndrome and كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية (excess fetal red erythroblasts). The inverse_isa relationship indicates that respiratory distress syndrome is a subtype of اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, while the has child relationship shows that كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية is a manifestation or sub‑condition within the same neonatal disorder. For each of these entities—اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, respiratory distress syndrome, and كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية—the qualifier “etiologie” is allowed, meaning that the cause or etiology can be specified for each condition. Recognizing and describing the etiological factors of the neonatal disorder and its constituent conditions is essential for accurate diagnosis and targeted management in newborn care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1691
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation is recognized as both the specific medical procedure and the category that defines it, effectively classifying itself within the domain of airway management and ventilatory support.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2395
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory distress syndrome is categorized as a type of اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ (respiratory disorder) and also as a form of اِضطِراب الرِّئَة (lung disorder). Respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult) is likewise placed under the broader category of اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ. These hierarchical relationships indicate that both respiratory distress syndrome and adult respiratory failure represent specific manifestations within the general class of respiratory disorders, with respiratory distress syndrome additionally recognized as a specific lung disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1924
retrieved_docs.content: Acid‑base imbalance (اختلال التوازن الحمضي القاعدي) refers to any disturbance in the normal pH regulation of the blood, encompassing both acidosis and alkalosis of metabolic and respiratory origin. Respiratory acidosis (تَقَلْوُن تنفسي) is a specific subtype of this broader category, occurring when hypoventilation leads to carbon dioxide retention, resulting in a decrease in blood pH. Understanding that respiratory acidosis is a form of acid‑base imbalance is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate therapeutic interventions, and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2146
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, indicating that certain respiratory interventions that are not directly therapeutic to the larynx can nonetheless involve the larynx indirectly. This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing the larynx’s involvement when planning and performing non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, as indirect effects on the larynx may impact airway function and patient outcomes.
